title: Plant tissues — how a leaf is built to do its job
examTip: AQA regularly asks you to explain why a leaf is well adapted for photosynthesis, and to describe what stomata and guard cells do. Know each tissue by name and function — and be ready to compare plant and animal cells.
introTips: This lesson covers how plant cells differ from animal cells, and how different tissues in a leaf each play a specific role.
You'll be able to explain what stomata and guard cells do and why they matter for controlling water loss.
AQA often asks you to explain leaf adaptations for photosynthesis — we'll practise linking structure to function throughout.
intro: A leaf looks simple — a flat green shape on a stem. But inside, it's a precision-engineered organ. Every layer has a specific job, every feature is there for a reason. The palisade cells near the top are packed with chloroplasts to catch the most light. The spongy layer below is full of air spaces to let gases move. Tiny pores called stomata open and close to manage water loss. Understanding plant tissues is really understanding how a plant balances two competing needs: making food, and not drying out.

diagrams.title: Cross-Section of a Leaf — Tissues and Cells
Stomata and Guard Cells — Open and Closed

keyPoints.heading: Plant cells vs animal cells — what's different
keyPoints.content: Plant cells share some features with animal cells — both have a nucleus, cytoplasm, cell membrane, mitochondria, and ribosomes. But plant cells have three extra structures. A rigid cell wall made of cellulose — it supports the cell and stops it bursting. A large central vacuole filled with cell sap — it keeps the cell turgid (firm). And in green parts of the plant, chloroplasts containing chlorophyll to absorb light for photosynthesis. Animal cells have none of these three.
writeBullets: Both plant and animal cells: nucleus, cytoplasm, cell membrane, mitochondria, ribosomes
Plant cells ONLY: cell wall (cellulose), large vacuole (cell sap), chloroplasts (in green parts)
Cell wall — support and structure; vacuole — turgidity; chloroplasts — photosynthesis
Animal cells do NOT have a cell wall, large vacuole, or chloroplasts

keyTerms.def: A rigid layer of cellulose surrounding plant cells; provides support and prevents the cell from bursting
A large, permanent fluid-filled sac in plant cells; contains cell sap and keeps the cell firm (turgid)
An organelle containing chlorophyll; the site of photosynthesis in plant cells

keyPoints.memTip: The three plant-only extras spell WVC: Wall, Vacuole, Chloroplast. Or remember: plants have Wall-to-Wall Vegetation with Chloroplasts. Say 'WVC' before every question about plant vs animal cells.

keyPoints.heading: Plant tissues — each layer has one job
keyPoints.content: A plant leaf is made of several distinct tissues, each with a specific role. The epidermal tissue covers the outside — it's a protective layer with a waxy cuticle on top to reduce water loss. Just below is the palisade mesophyll — tall, tightly packed cells crammed with chloroplasts to absorb as much light as possible. Below that is the spongy mesophyll — loosely packed cells with large air spaces between them for gas exchange. Running through the leaf are xylem and phloem — xylem carries water and minerals up from the roots; phloem carries sugars up and down the plant.
writeBullets: Epidermal tissue: outer protective layer; waxy cuticle reduces water loss
Palisade mesophyll: packed with chloroplasts near the top of the leaf — maximises light absorption
Spongy mesophyll: loose cells with air spaces — allows CO₂ and O₂ to move around inside the leaf
Xylem: carries water and dissolved minerals upward (transpiration stream)
Phloem: carries dissolved sugars up and down (translocation)

keyTerms.def: The outer protective layer of a plant organ; covered by a waxy cuticle in leaves
A layer of tall, chloroplast-rich cells near the top of a leaf; the main site of photosynthesis
A loosely packed layer with large air spaces for gas exchange inside the leaf
Plant tissue that transports water and dissolved minerals from roots to leaves
Plant tissue that transports dissolved sugars from leaves to the rest of the plant (translocation)

keyPoints.memTip: From top to bottom of a leaf: E-P-S-XP — Epidermis, Palisade, Spongy, Xylem and Phloem. Or make a story: 'Excellent Pandas Sit eXamining Physics' (Epidermis, Palisade, Spongy, Xylem, Phloem). Draw the cross-section from memory once and label it — that beats reading the list five times.

keyPoints.heading: Leaf adaptations — why a leaf is shaped the way it is
keyPoints.content: The leaf is optimised for photosynthesis, and its physical features all help. It's thin and flat — this gives a large surface area relative to volume, so more light can be captured and gases don't have far to travel. The large surface area means more light hits the leaf at once. The waxy cuticle on the upper epidermis is transparent (so light can pass through) and waterproof (so water doesn't evaporate from the surface). Stomata on the lower surface allow CO₂ in and O₂ out. Veins (xylem and phloem) reach every cell, supplying water and removing sugars.
writeBullets: Thin and flat: large surface area to volume ratio — more light absorbed, short diffusion distances
Waxy cuticle: transparent to let light through; waterproof to reduce evaporation
Stomata (lower surface): let CO₂ in for photosynthesis; O₂ and water vapour out
Network of veins (xylem + phloem): supply water to cells, remove products

keyTerms.def: A waxy waterproof layer on the upper epidermis of a leaf that reduces water loss while allowing light through
Tiny pores on the underside of leaves through which gas exchange occurs

keyPoints.memTip: For leaf adaptations, think about what a photosynthesising cell actually needs: Light (flat + large SA catches it), Water (xylem delivers it, cuticle conserves it), CO₂ (stomata let it in). Link every adaptation to one of those three needs and you'll never miss a mark.

keyPoints.heading: Stomata and guard cells — the leaf's control valves
keyPoints.content: Stomata (singular: stoma) are pores on the underside of leaves. Each stoma is flanked by two guard cells that control whether it's open or closed. In light, guard cells absorb water by osmosis and become turgid (swollen). Because their inner walls are thicker than their outer walls, they curve apart, opening the pore — CO₂ enters for photosynthesis, and O₂ and water vapour escape. In the dark, guard cells lose water, become flaccid, and straighten — closing the pore. This limits water loss at night when photosynthesis can't happen anyway.
writeBullets: Guard cells control stomata: turgid (swollen with water) → pore opens; flaccid (water lost) → pore closes
Light: guard cells take in water → turgid → stomata open → CO₂ in, O₂ and water vapour out
Dark: guard cells lose water → flaccid → stomata close → limits water loss at night
Guard cell inner wall is thicker — causes bowing/curving when turgid, opening the pore

keyTerms.def: Pairs of cells flanking each stoma; become turgid in light to open the pore, flaccid in dark to close it
Swollen and firm due to water entering by osmosis; guard cells are turgid when stomata are open
Limp due to water leaving by osmosis; guard cells are flaccid when stomata are closed
A pore in the leaf epidermis through which gases and water vapour are exchanged (plural: stomata)

keyPoints.memTip: Turgid = open, Flaccid = shut. Light makes guard cells turgid (they photosynthesize too — they actively absorb water when light is available). Dark makes them flaccid — they deflate and the pore slams shut. Make a fist for 'flaccid' (closed), then open your hand for 'turgid' (open). Do it while saying the words out loud — it sticks.

keyPoints.heading: Meristems — where plants grow
keyPoints.content: Unlike animals, plants don't grow all over — they grow only at specific zones called meristems. Meristems are found at the tips of roots and shoots (called apical meristems), and in some plants at the sides of stems (lateral meristems, responsible for girth). Meristem cells are undifferentiated — they keep dividing by mitosis to produce new cells, which then differentiate into the various plant tissues. This is why you can take a cutting from a stem tip and grow a whole new plant — the meristem cells are pluripotent.
writeBullets: Meristems: regions of actively dividing (mitosis) cells at root and shoot tips
Cells produced by meristems differentiate into different plant tissues
Apical meristems: at the tips of roots and shoots — responsible for length growth
Plants only grow in length from the tips, not along the whole stem

keyTerms.def: A region of undifferentiated, actively dividing cells at the tips of roots and shoots; responsible for plant growth
A meristem at the tip of a root or shoot; divides to produce cells that differentiate into plant tissues

keyPoints.memTip: MERistem = at the MERE TIP. Roots and shoots grow from the tip — cut the tip off and the plant stops growing that way. Think of a pencil: it only writes (grows) from the sharp end.

tables.title: Plant vs animal cells — features compared
Plant tissues — location and function
rows: Protection; waxy cuticle reduces water loss
Top of leaf (just below upper epidermis)
Photosynthesis — packed with chloroplasts to absorb light
Gas exchange — air spaces allow CO₂ and O₂ to circulate
Transports water and minerals upward from roots
Transports dissolved sugars (up and down — translocation)

2.question: Which tissue is responsible for most photosynthesis in a leaf?
2.correctFeedback: Correct — palisade mesophyll cells are tall and packed with chloroplasts, positioned near the top of the leaf to absorb maximum light.
2.wrongFeedback: The palisade mesophyll (option C) is the main site of photosynthesis. Spongy mesophyll handles gas exchange; epidermal tissue is protective; xylem carries water but doesn't photosynthesise.

4.question: What happens to stomata when guard cells become flaccid (lose water)?
4.correctFeedback: Yes — when guard cells lose water and become flaccid, they straighten and collapse together, closing the stoma. This happens in the dark to limit water loss.
4.wrongFeedback: Flaccid guard cells close the stoma (option B). When they're turgid (swollen with water) the pore opens; when they're flaccid (water lost) the pore closes. This is the plant's way of conserving water at night.

commonMistakes: Plant cells have a cell wall AND a cell membrane — both are present. The cell wall is outside the membrane, made of cellulose. They are not the same thing.
Chloroplasts are only found in the GREEN parts of a plant — not in root cells, which are underground and never see light. Root cells have no chloroplasts.
Xylem carries water and minerals UPWARD only (one-way flow). Phloem carries sugars UP AND DOWN — this is called translocation, and the direction depends on where the plant needs energy.
Stomata are on the UNDERSIDE (lower epidermis) of most leaves — not the top. This reduces direct sun exposure and limits evaporation.
Guard cells open stomata in LIGHT, not warmth. The trigger is light availability (for photosynthesis), not temperature.
examTips: For 'explain how a leaf is adapted for photosynthesis' — always mention surface area, thin shape, chloroplasts in palisade cells, and stomata for gas exchange. Each one needs a reason, not just a name.

Guard cell questions often ask you to 'explain' the mechanism — say that water enters by osmosis (turgid), causing the uneven cell wall to bow outward and open the pore. The thick inner wall is the structural key.
Don't confuse xylem and phloem in terms of direction. Xylem is one-way (up), phloem is two-way. AQA sometimes tests this with a question about 'which vessel carries dissolved sugars'.

revisionCardBullets: Plant-only extras: WVC — Wall (cellulose), Vacuole (cell sap), Chloroplasts (green parts only)
Animal cells have all the same basic structures — but NO cell wall, NO large vacuole, NO chloroplasts
Leaf tissue layers top to bottom: Epidermis → Palisade mesophyll → Spongy mesophyll → Xylem/Phloem
Palisade mesophyll: packed with chloroplasts, near top of leaf — main photosynthesis zone
Spongy mesophyll: air spaces between cells — allows gas exchange inside the leaf
Xylem: water and minerals UP (one-way); Phloem: sugars up and down (translocation)
Leaf adaptations: thin + flat (large SA, short diffusion distances), waxy cuticle (transparent + waterproof), stomata (gas exchange)
Guard cells turgid (light) → stomata OPEN; guard cells flaccid (dark) → stomata CLOSED
Meristems at root and shoot TIPS — only place where plant cells divide to grow in length
Stomata are on the LOWER epidermis — reduces direct sun exposure and water loss
